Physical Address: H.E.J. Research Institute of Chemistry, International Centre for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS), University of Karachi, Karachi 75270, Pakistan
Evidence Text: The CAP for PMMA has been reported using different mobile phases on various stationary phases of different nature. Selection of CAP for a particular polymer depends upon the segment that needs to be analyzed under such conditions. In current study, the other block PEO is polar than PMMA. On RP column, PEOs are expected to be excluded from the stationary phase at CAP of PMMA. Another aspect that needs to be considered is the solubility of all the segments of the polymer under study in the mobile phase. Taking into account above considerations, a ternary mixture of ACN, THF, and TEA has been selected. ACN is adsorption promoting solvent under such conditions whereas THF promotes desorption. Addition of 1.0% TEA is crucial in order to avoid unwanted polar interactions of ether groups with the unmodiﬁed silanol groups of the stationary phase. PMMA41k eluted later than PMMA2k while using mobile phase composed of ACN/THF/TEA: 94/5/1 v/v, showing slight adsorption of PMMA repeat unit, Fig. 4A. Increasing relative content of THF in the mobile phase to 9% reversed the elution order, PMMA41k eluted earlier than PMMA2k, a typical size exclusion behaviour, Fig. 4B. There must be a mobile phase composition between above-mentioned compositions that should result in molar mass independent elution of PMMAs. In mobile phase composition of ACN/THF/TEA: 93/6/1 v/v, all PMMAs eluted at the same elution volume irrespective of large difference in their molar masses, Fig. 4C. These conditions are termed as liquid chromatography at critical conditions (LCCC) or critical adsorption point (CAP). Under such conditions, other polymers show their own particular elution behaviour. The other block in current study, PEO is polar than PMMA hence elute in size exclusion regime earlier than the critical point of PMMAs, Fig. 4D. The conditions can be utilized for block copolymers of PMMA with any other polymer provided the noncritical polymer is soluble in the mobile phase. The block copolymer should be separated with regard to the other block, and PMMA homopolymer should be separated from block copolymer.

Evidence Text: In LACCC mode a polymer elutes independently of its molar mass according to its chemical heterogeneity, e.p. end groups. This particular mode is nearly exclusively applied in the 1° dimension of the 2D chromatography. By means of a switching valve fractions are taken and transferred into the 2™ dimension, which is SEC. Using commercially available software (WINGPC™: Polymer Standard Service, Mainz, Germany) 2D plots can be created showing molar mass versus end group distribution. The analysis of copolymers can also be performed without this often Lime-consuming combination of methods. First the ‘critical’ conditions of separation of one polymer unit have to be ascertained. At these conditions separation according to the mass of the second polymer unit must be achieved. The same procedure, ie. separation at ‘critical’ conditions, has also to be performed for the second polymer unit. After calibration of SEC for both units the molar mass and molar mass distribution (MMD) of the copolymer units can be obtained within a certain mass range, which is different for various polymer systems. An example for a PEO-a-PPo mono-n-butyl ether polymer is shown in Figs. 1(a)—-1(d). Hence, one has to ascertain ‘critical’ conditions of adsorption for each single copolymer structure and, simultaneously, to ensure a separation according to molar mass for the corresponding second polymer unit. This cannot be achieved for all copolymers. Sometimes, at ‘critical’ conditions for the first unit, the second unit elutes in adsorption mode, and a SEC separation cannot be obtained. Furthermore, especially at higher masses, the solubility of one structural unit at ‘critical’ conditions of the other is limited, which results in precipitation at the top of the separation columns. Furthermore, the separation at ‘critical’ conditions of adsorption is achieved in most cases using binary (or ternary) solvent mixtures, which might contain salt to avoid aggregation. Thus, the transfer of fractions from LACCC mode to SEC mode can lead to strong additional signals in the size exclusion chromatograms. In conjunction with a drastically lower polymer concentration in the second dimension due to fractionation (up to 1M) single fractions) of the original amount of sample, clear peak detection cannot always be assured. Another important drawback is the lack of standards for calibrating the SEC. Evidence Text: A further increase of the MeOH content reverses the elution order with higher molar mass samples eluting at lower elution times than those of lower molar mass, i.e. an SEC like elution order is observed. Only the PM is capable to describe these two modes of chromatography. However, at MeOH contents of more than 90% the retention times decrease again and the different curves merge once more at a MeOH a content of approximately 98%, indicating a second Ga» critical point. Such a behaviour for PEGs has been found for other mobile phases on RP stationary phases as well and has been attributed to the interaction of residual silanol groups of the stationary phase with the oxygen atoms of the PEO chain, while the ethylene structures interact with the C18-chains in the usual reverse phase mode [20,21].

In acetone-water, there is also a second CAP at about 92% acetone. As described previously, we have separated Tween 40 and 60 on a RP column at the CAP for PEG in methanol – water (Figure 2). In this mobile phase, the retention of the hydrophobic groups is very high, which allows a separation of the monoesters, while the diesters should elute quite late. Evidently, Tween 40 contains two types of monoesters (presumably the monopalmitates of ethoxylated sorbitan and isosorbide). The same peaks appear also in Tween 60 (as expected).

In acetone-water, there is also a second CAP at about 92% acetone. As described previously, we have separated Tween 40 and 60 on a RP column at the CAP for PEG in methanol – water (Figure 2). In this mobile phase, the retention of the hydrophobic groups is very high, which allows a separation of the monoesters, while the diesters should elute quite late. Evidently, Tween 40 contains two types of monoesters (presumably the monopalmitates of ethoxylated sorbitan and isosorbide). The same peaks appear also in Tween 60 (as expected).

As has already been mentioned above, a second CAP for PEG exists on typical reversed phase columns in acetone – water mobile phases containing about 92 wt-% acetone. In such a mobile phase, the retention of fatty esters is much lower than in 90 % methanol, consequently, the monoesters will elute quite early, and the diesters should elute in a reasonable time. The separation pattern of polysorbate 40 and 60 under such conditions is shown in Figure 7. Monoesters are eluted as one peak, while the diesters show a completely different behaviour, which is predicted by the theory: at the CAP for the center block (B), difunctional chains and A-B-A triblocks with adsorbing end groups should elute in SEC-like order and not as a sharp peak (which is the case for nonfunctional homopolymers and monofunctional polymers or A-B diblocks). The effect is, of course, even more pronounced for stars with three or more adsorbing end groups (such as the tri- and tetraesters of ethoxylated sorbitan).

As has already been shown in a previous paper, critical conditions for PEG exist on typical reversed phase columns also in ternary mobile phases consisting of acetone, methanol, and water. In such phases, there is a critical adsorption line (CAL) connecting the CAPs in the binary mobile phases. In order to obtain information on the composition of the monoester fraction as well, we have now applied gradient elution from one critical point to another one. When we ran a gradient from such a ternary mobile phase to the (second) CAP in acetone – water (92% acetone), a very nice separation of the individual fractions could be achieved. For the identification of the individual peaks we compared the chromatograms with those of the products obtained by esterification of Tween 40, PEG 600 and hexa-EG with palmitic acid.

Evidence Text: The CAP of PS was realised in a DMF–THF mobile phase on a RP octadecyl column. This allowed us to extend the molar mass range up to the hundred thousands. As demonstrated in Fig. 1a, PS calibration standards adsorb in 100% DMF on a silica-based octadecyl column. The higher molar mass standards elute later than the lower molar mass standards. The elution volume decreases as the amount of THF in the mobile phase is increased. A point is reached at which all PS standards elute at more or less the same elution volume irrespective of their molar masses (THF–DMF: 18–82). These conditions are called the critical conditions or the CAP of PS for the specific column, temperature and mobile phase conditions used.

Evidence Text: The CAP of PEO was realised on a normal phase Si column in the same mobile phase system (THF–DMF) which is used for the critical conditions of PS. THF is an adsorption promoting solvent for PEOs on a normal phase Si column. The addition of DMF promotes desorption. At a mobile phase composition of 96–4 (THF–DMF, vol.%), all PEO standards elute at more or less the same elution volume and show no molar mass dependence. Further increase in the DMF content in the mobile phase leads to the exclusion of PEOs from pores of the stationary phase resulting in earlier elution of higher molar mass standards than the lower molar mass standards.

Evidence Text: The critical diagram M vs. retention time for a RP-18 column of 60 mm length is shown in Figure 1. At acetonitrile concentrations >47% by volume in the solvent mixture, the retention time decreases as the molar mass of the PEO calibration sample increases; per definition retention corresponds to a size-exclusion mode. The reverse behaviour is obtained at acetonitrile concentrations <45% by volume, where the retention time increases with increasing molar mass. At a solvent composition of acetonitrile-water 46:54% by volume, the retention time does not change with molar mass, and at this "critical point of adsorption" separation is accomplished exclusively with respect to functionality regardless of the molar mass.

Evidence Text: The CAP for PMMA has been reported using different mobile phases on various stationary phases of different nature. Selection of CAP for a particular polymer depends upon the segment that needs to be analyzed under such conditions. In current study, the other block PEO is polar than PMMA. On RP column, PEOs are expected to be excluded from the stationary phase at CAP of PMMA. Another aspect that needs to be considered is the solubility of all the segments of the polymer under study in the mobile phase. Taking into account above considerations, a ternary mixture of ACN, THF, and TEA has been selected. ACN is adsorption promoting solvent under such conditions whereas THF promotes desorption. Addition of 1.0% TEA is crucial in order to avoid unwanted polar interactions of ether groups with the unmodiﬁed silanol groups of the stationary phase. PMMA41k eluted later than PMMA2k while using mobile phase composed of ACN/THF/TEA: 94/5/1 v/v, showing slight adsorption of PMMA repeat unit, Fig. 4A. Increasing relative content of THF in the mobile phase to 9% reversed the elution order, PMMA41k eluted earlier than PMMA2k, a typical size exclusion behaviour, Fig. 4B. There must be a mobile phase composition between above-mentioned compositions that should result in molar mass independent elution of PMMAs. In mobile phase composition of ACN/THF/TEA: 93/6/1 v/v, all PMMAs eluted at the same elution volume irrespective of large difference in their molar masses, Fig. 4C. These conditions are termed as liquid chromatography at critical conditions (LCCC) or critical adsorption point (CAP). Under such conditions, other polymers show their own particular elution behaviour. The other block in current study, PEO is polar than PMMA hence elute in size exclusion regime earlier than the critical point of PMMAs, Fig. 4D. The conditions can be utilized for block copolymers of PMMA with any other polymer provided the noncritical polymer is soluble in the mobile phase. The block copolymer should be separated with regard to the other block, and PMMA homopolymer should be separated from block copolymer.

There must be a mobile phase composition between above-mentioned compositions that should result in molar mass independent elution of PMMAs. In mobile phase composition of ACN/THF/TEA: 93/6/1 v/v, all PMMAs eluted at the same elution volume irrespective of large difference in their molar masses, Fig. 4C. These conditions are termed as liquid chromatography at critical conditions (LCCC) or critical adsorption point (CAP). Under such conditions, other polymers show their own particular elution behaviour. The other block in current study, PEO is polar than PMMA hence elute in size exclusion regime earlier than the critical point of PMMAs, Fig. 4D. The conditions can be utilized for block copolymers of PMMA with any other polymer provided the noncritical polymer is soluble in the mobile phase. The block copolymer should be separated with regard to the other block, and PMMA homopolymer should be separated from block copolymer.

Evidence Text: The CAP for PMMA has been reported using different mobile phases on various stationary phases of different nature. Selection of CAP for a particular polymer depends upon the segment that needs to be analyzed under such conditions. In current study, the other block PEO is polar than PMMA. On RP column, PEOs are expected to be excluded from the stationary phase at CAP of PMMA. Another aspect that needs to be considered is the solubility of all the segments of the polymer under study in the mobile phase. Taking into account above considerations, a ternary mixture of ACN, THF, and TEA has been selected. ACN is adsorption promoting solvent under such conditions whereas THF promotes desorption. Addition of 1.0% TEA is crucial in order to avoid unwanted polar interactions of ether groups with the unmodiﬁed silanol groups of the stationary phase. PMMA41k eluted later than PMMA2k while using mobile phase composed of ACN/THF/TEA: 94/5/1 v/v, showing slight adsorption of PMMA repeat unit, Fig. 4A. The other block in current study, PEO is polar than PMMA hence elute in size exclusion regime earlier than the critical point of PMMAs, Fig. 4D. Addition of 1.0% TEA is crucial in order to avoid unwanted polar interactions of ether groups with the unmodiﬁed silanol groups of the stationary phase. PMMA41k eluted later than PMMA2k while using mobile phase composed of ACN/THF/TEA: 94/5/1 v/v, showing slight adsorption of PMMA repeat unit, Fig. 4A. Increasing relative content of THF in the mobile phase to 9% reversed the elution order, PMMA41k eluted earlier than PMMA2k, a typical size exclusion behaviour, Fig. 4B. There must be a mobile phase composition between above-mentioned compositions that should result in molar mass independent elution of PMMAs. In mobile phase composition of ACN/THF/TEA: 93/6/1 v/v, all PMMAs eluted at the same elution volume irrespective of large difference in their molar masses, Fig. 4C. These conditions are termed as liquid chromatography at critical conditions (LCCC) or critical adsorption point (CAP). Under such conditions, other polymers show their own particular elution behaviour. The other block in current study, PEO is polar than PMMA hence elute in size exclusion regime earlier than the critical point of PMMAs, Fig. 4D. The conditions can be utilized for block copolymers of PMMA with any other polymer provided the noncritical polymer is soluble in the mobile phase. The block copolymer should be separated with regard to the other block, and PMMA homopolymer should be separated from block copolymer.

In acetone-water, there is also a second CAP at about 92% acetone. As described previously, we have separated Tween 40 and 60 on a RP column at the CAP for PEG in methanol – water (Figure 2). In this mobile phase, the retention of the hydrophobic groups is very high, which allows a separation of the monoesters, while the diesters should elute quite late. Evidently, Tween 40 contains two types of monoesters (presumably the monopalmitates of ethoxylated sorbitan and isosorbide). The same peaks appear also in Tween 60 (as expected).

In acetone-water, there is also a second CAP at about 92% acetone. As described previously, we have separated Tween 40 and 60 on a RP column at the CAP for PEG in methanol – water (Figure 2). In this mobile phase, the retention of the hydrophobic groups is very high, which allows a separation of the monoesters, while the diesters should elute quite late. Evidently, Tween 40 contains two types of monoesters (presumably the monopalmitates of ethoxylated sorbitan and isosorbide). The same peaks appear also in Tween 60 (as expected).

As has already been mentioned above, a second CAP for PEG exists on typical reversed phase columns in acetone – water mobile phases containing about 92 wt-% acetone. In such a mobile phase, the retention of fatty esters is much lower than in 90 % methanol, consequently, the monoesters will elute quite early, and the diesters should elute in a reasonable time. The separation pattern of polysorbate 40 and 60 under such conditions is shown in Figure 7. Monoesters are eluted as one peak, while the diesters show a completely different behaviour, which is predicted by the theory: at the CAP for the center block (B), difunctional chains and A-B-A triblocks with adsorbing end groups should elute in SEC-like order and not as a sharp peak (which is the case for nonfunctional homopolymers and monofunctional polymers or A-B diblocks). The effect is, of course, even more pronounced for stars with three or more adsorbing end groups (such as the tri- and tetraesters of ethoxylated sorbitan).

As has already been shown in a previous paper, critical conditions for PEG exist on typical reversed phase columns also in ternary mobile phases consisting of acetone, methanol, and water. In such phases, there is a critical adsorption line (CAL) connecting the CAPs in the binary mobile phases. In order to obtain information on the composition of the monoester fraction as well, we have now applied gradient elution from one critical point to another one. When we ran a gradient from such a ternary mobile phase to the (second) CAP in acetone – water (92% acetone), a very nice separation of the individual fractions could be achieved. For the identification of the individual peaks we compared the chromatograms with those of the products obtained by esterification of Tween 40, PEG 600 and hexa-EG with palmitic acid.

Evidence Text: At the transition point of these two mechanisms, which is often termed the “critical adsorption point” (CAP), there is a special mobile-phase composition (at a given temperature), where the interaction parameter has a value of zero: under such conditions, the polymer is eluted—independent of its molar mass—at the void volume of the column. In other words, at the CAP the polymer chain becomes chromatographically invisible [2–5]. This situation can be utilized in the characterization of functional oligomers, block and graft copolymers, as it allows the separation of polymer homologues according to other structural units, such as their end groups or other blocks [3, 6– 11] (M.I. Malik, B. Trathnigg, and C.O. Kappe, unpublished results). Especially interesting are amphiphilic polymers, which contain a hydrophilic block [such as poly(ethylene oxide)] linked to a hydrophobic block [such as poly(propylene oxide), a higher poly(alkylene oxide), and polycaprolactone, PCL]. These materials have applications in many fields, such as nonionic surfactants and emulsifiers, in which the hydrophilic (water-soluble) polyoxyethylene chain is linked to lipophilic (oil-soluble) groups or blocks. Hence, these products are surface-active: they arrange themselves at the surface of water or at the oil–water interface and thus lower the surface tension. Amphiphilic block copolymers of ε-caprolactone and ethylene oxide (EO) have special properties [12]. As the poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG) block minimizes platelet deposition onto surfaces exposed to the bloodstream [13], they can be used to produce blood-contacting implant devices having low protein adsorption, membranes for separation processes, biosensors, chromatographic supports, contact lenses, and in blood and protein storage applications, etc. [14]. These polymers may contain various impurities (such as the homopolymers, residual monomer, and initiator), which may influence the properties of the products. Consequently, there is a strong need for reliable characterization methods. Owing to the complex nature of amphiphilic polymers, this will also require complex techniques, such as two-dimensional liquid chromatography. There have also been attempts to apply pyrolysis gas chromatography [15, 16] and mass spectroscopy [mainly matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time of flight (MALDI-TOF) mass spectroscopy] [17– 19]. Pyrolysis gas chromatography is very useful in the analysis of cross-linked polymers, but it cannot be considered as a standard technique in the characterization of block copolymers: since 2000, there have been very few papers in the literature. MALDI-TOF mass spectroscopy is problematic in the analysis of highly polydisperse samples; it works very well, however, with samples with a narrow molar mass distribution. When applied to fractions from a chromatographic separation, it yields highly valuable information. In liquid chromatography, one may use different separation modes, which separate species according to different criteria (corresponding to the interaction parameters of the components), and combine them to achieve a two-dimensional separation. In the case of block copolymers, the interaction parameters cA and cB of the individual structural units (A and B) will typically have different values. If both cA and cB are negative, the overall molar mass distribution can be determined by SEC. In SEC, retention decreases with increasing molar mass and shows only a minor influence of chemical composition. It must be mentioned that the SEC calibration functions of different polymers can be considerably different (as may be the interaction parameters). In this case the chemical composition along the peak is required, which can be obtained from dual detection [20–22]. If both cA and cB are positive, a reasonable separation by LAC (with retention increasing exponentially with the number of repeat units) can only be achieved if one block is monodisperse [6, 23]. If, however, both blocks are polydisperse and cA ≠ cB, several superimposed series of peaks will be obtained, and these cannot be resolved. If one of the interaction parameters for one repeat unit is positive and the other one is negative, each polymer homologue is eluted in SEC order, but far behind the void volume of the column. This mechanism, which is called “liquid exclusion–adsorption chromatography” (LEAC) [24, 25], can be utilized in the separation of monofunctional oligomers with a monodisperse adsorbing block or end group. In the analysis of block copolymers, LEAC may only be applied as the second dimension in two-dimensional chromatography. If one of the interaction parameters (cA or cB) is zero, the corresponding block becomes “chromatographically invisible” and separation according to the other block can be achieved (liquid chromatography under critical conditions, LCCC). Depending on the interaction parameter of the other repeat unit, this separation may follow a SEC [7, 26–28] (situation A) or a LAC [6, 9, 29, 30] mechanism (situation B). There could be different options, as has been discussed previously [31, 32] (see Table 1). On reversed-phase columns, there may be critical conditions for the hydrophilic block in a mobile phase with rather high polarity. In such a mobile phase the hydrophobic block will be eluted in LAC mode. On the other hand, critical conditions may also be found for the hydrophobic block in a rather nonpolar mobile phase. In this case, the hydrophilic block will be eluted in SEC mode. On normal-phase columns, a CAP for the hydrophilic block may be found in a polar mobile phase, in which the hydrophobic block will be eluted in SEC mode. In a mobile phase with low polarity, a CAP may exist for the hydrophobic block: in this case the hydrophilic block will be eluted in LAC mode. In this study these possible situations were evaluated with respect of advantages and limitations. As practical examples — we chose various block copolymers based on epoxide EO, propylene oxide (PO), butene oxide (BO), hexene oxide — (HO), lactone (caprolactone, CL) repeat units or polymers with terminal alkyl groups. These samples were analyzed on various columns of different nature and in different mobile phases.

Such a behaviour for PEGs has been found for other mobile phases on RP stationary phases as well and has been attributed to the interaction of residual silanol groups of the stationary phase with the oxygen atoms of the PEO chain, while the ethylene structures interact with the C18-chains in the usual reverse phase mode [20,21].

The critical conditions of PS was established using a C18 modified stationary phase with THF (good solvent, which promotes desorption of the sample from C18 modified stationary phase) and H2O (poor solvent, which promotes adsorption of the sample to the C18 modified stationary phase) as mobile phase composition. Different ratios of THF:H2O from (87:13 vol.%) to (90:10 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Pre-selected PS calibration standards were dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The results for the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.6. As can be seen the PS calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower elution volume (Ve)) when using a THF:H2O composition of 90:10 vol.%. When using THF: H2O 87:13 vol.% the PS calibration standards elute in the liquid adsorption chromatography (LAC) mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of THF:H2O 88.5:11.5 vol.% corresponds to the critical conditions. As can be observed, up to a molecular weight at the peak maximum (Mp) of 33000 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve, in other words at the critical conditions. A Mp of 33000 g/mol was the highest molecular weight to be used because the stationary phase had a small average pore size of 100 Å and an exclusion limit of about 30000 g/mol. However, solubility in this solvent composition was achieved for much higher molecular weights.

When using 100 vol.% DMF the PS calibration standards elute in LAC mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of 18:82 vol.% (THF:DMF) was the closest to the critical conditions that could be obtained. As can be observed, up to a Mp of 39200 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve. For the higher molecular weights Ve decreases slightly indicating that these standards do not elute exactly at critical conditions. For this study, however, a further adjustment of the critical conditions was not conducted.

For the critical conditions of PEO, DMF (good solvent, which promotes desorption of sample from silica based stationary phase) and THF (poor solvent, which promotes adsorption of sample to a silica based stationary phase) was used as mobile phase. From the work of Berek[5] the approximate ratio of the two solvents at the critical conditions of PEO was known. Different ratios of DMF:THF from (50:50 vol.%) to (0:100 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Preselected PEO calibration standards where dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The resulting log (Mp) vs. Ve plots using the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.36. As can be seen the PEO calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower Ve) when using a DMF:THF composition of 50:50 vol.%. When using 100 vol.% THF the PEO calibration standards elute in LAC mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of 4:96 vol.% (DMF:THF) was the closest to the critical conditions that could be obtained. As can be observed, up to a Mp of 114000 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve. The PEO calibration standards used are from two manufacturers and, therefore, might slightly vary in chemical composition due to different synthetic procedures (see Table 3.2). The slight variation in critical elution volume for the PEO standard 23600 g/mol might indicate this situation.

The analysis of the copolymers using LCCC of PS was followed by two-dimensional liquid chromatography (2D-LC) analysis of the copolymers. Critical conditions of PS were used in the first dimension and SEC using DMF as the eluent for the second dimension. DMF was used as the eluent for the second dimension because in pure THF the block copolymer samples could not be completely dissolved, not even upon heating. The PS calibration curve (Figure 3.1) was applied to the obtained 2D data. For comparison the original data with an elution volume axis are shown in Figure 4.31. The resulting 2D plots are shown below. When looking at the plot, for example of PS-b-PEO 1 (Figure 4.26), the y-axis shows the LCCC of PS separation (separation according to chemical composition) and the x-axis shows the SEC separation (separation according to size) to which the PS calibration curve was applied in order to obtain a molar mass scale. The black dots are just defects, but where difficult to remove without compromising the actual 2D-plots (encircled) of the samples.

The analysis of the copolymers using LCCC of PEO was followed by 2D-LC analysis of the copolymers. Critical conditions of PEO were used in the first dimension and as the second dimension SEC using DMF as the mobile phase for the second dimension. The resulting 2D plots are shown below. When looking at the plots for PS-b-PEO 1 to PS-b-PEO 3 (Figure 4.49 to Figure 4.51) a slight increase in molecular weight can be observed for the latter. For PS-b-PEO 4 and PS-b-PEO 8 (Figure 4.52 to Figure 4.57) copolymer and PEO homopolymer peaks can be seen. The molecular weight for the PEO homopolymers in the latter four samples is quite low compared to the result of the molecular weight for the copolymers at the critical conditions of PS but a slight increase can be observed. PS-b-PEO 4 has the highest molecular weight PEO homopolymer. Figure 4.54 is the same 2D plot for PS-b-PEO 6 except not PEO calibration was applied and the original data are supplied.

Evidence Text: In the case of amphiphilic polymers the interaction parameters cA and cB of the individual structural units (A and B) may have different values. If both cA and cB are negative, the molar mass distribution (MMD) can be determined by size exclusion chromatography (SEC), with retention decreasing with increasing molar mass, and only a minor influence of chemical composition. SEC yields the overall MMD, with dual detection [54–56] one may obtain information on the chemical composition along the MMD [56–58]. SEC is however not capable of discriminating a mixture of block copolymers and homopolymers. In the case of higher polymers, gradient LAC [59–66] or LC under limiting conditions [67–69] can be applied. In the range of lower molecular mass, however, LCCC is the best approach to separate homopolymers from the block copolymer. In AB diblock copolymers (e.g. A = EO and B = PO or another alkylene oxide) one may use the following strategies: At the CAP for the unit A, a separation according to the block B may be achieved by a SEC or a LAC mechanism, while at the CAP for the block B, the length of the block A may be obtained in the same way. The application of these methods to EO–PO block copolymers has been described recently [20,45,70–72].

The conditions used for the critical conditions of PS are as follows: a mobile phase composition of THF:DMF 18:82 vol.% with a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min. A C18 modified silica column was used (Symmetry 300 Å, 5 µm, 4.6 x 250 mm, Waters, Milford, MA, USA) at 30ºC. PS calibration standards were used to establish the critical conditions.

The conditions used for the critical conditions of PEO are as follows: a solvent composition of DMF:THF 4:96 vol.% with a flow rate of 0.5 mL/min. A silica column was used (Nucleosil 300 Å, 5 µm, 4.6 x 250 mm, Macherey-Nagel, Düren, Germany) at 29.7ºC. PEO calibration standards were used to establish the critical conditions.

The critical conditions of PS was established using a C18 modified stationary phase with THF (good solvent, which promotes desorption of the sample from C18 modified stationary phase) and H2O (poor solvent, which promotes adsorption of the sample to the C18 modified stationary phase) as mobile phase composition. Different ratios of THF:H2O from (87:13 vol.%) to (90:10 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Pre-selected PS calibration standards were dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The results for the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.6. As can be seen the PS calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower elution volume (Ve)) when using a THF:H2O composition of 90:10 vol.%. When using THF: H2O 87:13 vol.% the PS calibration standards elute in the liquid adsorption chromatography (LAC) mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of THF:H2O 88.5:11.5 vol.% corresponds to the critical conditions. As can be observed, up to a molecular weight at the peak maximum (Mp) of 33000 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve, in other words at the critical conditions. A Mp of 33000 g/mol was the highest molecular weight to be used because the stationary phase had a small average pore size of 100 Å and an exclusion limit of about 30000 g/mol. However, solubility in this solvent composition was achieved for much higher molecular weights.

For the present study a Symmetry C18 300 Å column was used and different ratios of THF:H2O from (87:13 vol.%) to (90:10 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Pre-selected PS calibration standards were dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The results for the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.6. As can be seen the PS calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower elution volume (Ve)) when using a THF:H2O composition of 90:10 vol.%. When using THF: H2O 87:13 vol.% the PS calibration standards elute in the liquid adsorption chromatography (LAC) mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of THF:H2O 88.5:11.5 vol.% corresponds to the critical conditions. As can be observed, up to a molecular weight at the peak maximum (Mp) of 33000 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve, in other words at the critical conditions. A Mp of 33000 g/mol was the highest molecular weight to be used because the stationary phase had a small average pore size of 100 Å and an exclusion limit of about 30000 g/mol. However, solubility in this solvent composition was achieved for much higher molecular weights.

The critical conditions of PS was established using a C18 modified stationary phase with THF (good solvent) and DMF (poor solvent) as mobile phase composition. Different ratios of THF:DMF from (20:80 vol.%) to (17:83 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Pre-selected PS calibration standards where dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The results of using the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.12. As can be seen the PS calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower Ve) when using a THF:DMF composition of 20:80 vol.%. When using 100 vol.% DMF the PS calibration standards elute in LAC mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of 18:82 vol.% (THF:DMF) was the closest to the critical conditions that could be obtained. As can be observed, up to a Mp of 39200 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve. For the higher molecular weights Ve decreases slightly indicating that these standards do not elute exactly at critical conditions. For this study, however, a further adjustment of the critical conditions was not conducted.

For the critical conditions of PEO, DMF (good solvent, which promotes desorption of sample from silica based stationary phase) and THF (poor solvent, which promotes adsorption of sample to a silica based stationary phase) was used as mobile phase. From the work of Berek[5] the approximate ratio of the two solvents at the critical conditions of PEO was known. Different ratios of DMF:THF from (50:50 vol.%) to (0:100 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Preselected PEO calibration standards where dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The resulting log (Mp) vs. Ve plots using the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.36. As can be seen the PEO calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower Ve) when using a DMF:THF composition of 50:50 vol.%. When using 100 vol.% THF the PEO calibration standards elute in LAC mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of 4:96 vol.% (DMF:THF) was the closest to the critical conditions that could be obtained. As can be observed, up to a Mp of 114000 g/mol the calibration standards elute at nearly the same Ve. The PEO calibration standards used are from two manufacturers and, therefore, might slightly vary in chemical composition due to different synthetic procedures (see Table 3.2). The slight variation in critical elution volume for the PEO standard 23600 g/mol might indicate this situation.

The analysis of the copolymers using LCCC of PS was followed by two-dimensional liquid chromatography (2D-LC) analysis of the copolymers. Critical conditions of PS were used in the first dimension and SEC using DMF as the eluent for the second dimension. DMF was used as the eluent for the second dimension because in pure THF the block copolymer samples could not be completely dissolved, not even upon heating. The PS calibration curve (Figure 3.1) was applied to the obtained 2D data. For comparison the original data with an elution volume axis are shown in Figure 4.31. The resulting 2D plots are shown below. When looking at the plot, for example of PS-b-PEO 1 (Figure 4.26), the y-axis shows the LCCC of PS separation (separation according to chemical composition) and the x-axis shows the SEC separation (separation according to size) to which the PS calibration curve was applied in order to obtain a molar mass scale. The black dots are just defects, but where difficult to remove without compromising the actual 2D-plots (encircled) of the samples.

The analysis of the copolymers using LCCC of PEO was followed by 2D-LC analysis of the copolymers. Critical conditions of PEO were used in the first dimension and as the second dimension SEC using DMF as the mobile phase for the second dimension. The resulting 2D plots are shown below. When looking at the plots for PS-b-PEO 1 to PS-b-PEO 3 (Figure 4.49 to Figure 4.51) a slight increase in molecular weight can be observed for the latter. For PS-b-PEO 4 and PS-b-PEO 8 (Figure 4.52 to Figure 4.57) copolymer and PEO homopolymer peaks can be seen. The molecular weight for the PEO homopolymers in the latter four samples is quite low compared to the result of the molecular weight for the copolymers at the critical conditions of PS but a slight increase can be observed. PS-b-PEO 4 has the highest molecular weight PEO homopolymer. Figure 4.54 is the same 2D plot for PS-b-PEO 6 except not PEO calibration was applied and the original data are supplied.

The critical conditions of PS were established using a silica based stationary phase with THF:DMF as mobile phase. From the work of Berek[5] the approximate ratio of the two solvents at the critical conditions of PS was known. Different ratios of DMF:THF from (50:50 vol.%) to (0:100 vol.%) were pre-mixed. Preselected PS calibration standards where dissolved in the different pre-mixed solvent compositions. The resulting log (Mp) vs. Ve plots using the different solvent compositions are shown in Figure 4.14. As can be seen the PS calibration standards elute in the SEC mode (lower Ve) when using a DMF:THF composition of 50:50 vol.%. When using 100 vol.% THF the PS calibration standards elute in LAC mode (higher Ve).The solvent composition of 18:82 vol.% (DMF:THF) was the closest to the critical conditions that could be obtained.

Evidence Text: The CAP for PMMA has been reported using different mobile phases on various stationary phases of different nature. Selection of CAP for a particular polymer depends upon the segment that needs to be analyzed under such conditions. In current study, the other block PEO is polar than PMMA. On RP column, PEOs are expected to be excluded from the stationary phase at CAP of PMMA. Another aspect that needs to be considered is the solubility of all the segments of the polymer under study in the mobile phase. Taking into account above considerations, a ternary mixture of ACN, THF, and TEA has been selected. ACN is adsorption promoting solvent under such conditions whereas THF promotes desorption. Addition of 1.0% TEA is crucial in order to avoid unwanted polar interactions of ether groups with the unmodiﬁed silanol groups of the stationary phase. PMMA41k eluted later than PMMA2k while using mobile phase composed of ACN/THF/TEA: 94/5/1 v/v, showing slight adsorption of PMMA repeat unit, Fig. 4A. Increasing relative content of THF in the mobile phase to 9% reversed the elution order, PMMA41k eluted earlier than PMMA2k, a typical size exclusion behaviour, Fig. 4B. There must be a mobile phase composition between above-mentioned compositions that should result in molar mass independent elution of PMMAs. In mobile phase composition of ACN/THF/TEA: 93/6/1 v/v, all PMMAs eluted at the same elution volume irrespective of large difference in their molar masses, Fig. 4C. These conditions are termed as liquid chromatography at critical conditions (LCCC) or critical adsorption point (CAP). Under such conditions, other polymers show their own particular elution behaviour. The other block in current study, PEO is polar than PMMA hence elute in size exclusion regime earlier than the critical point of PMMAs, Fig. 4D. The conditions can be utilized for block copolymers of PMMA with any other polymer provided the noncritical polymer is soluble in the mobile phase. The block copolymer should be separated with regard to the other block, and PMMA homopolymer should be separated from block copolymer.

Evidence Text: The CAP of PS was realised in a DMF–THF mobile phase on a RP octadecyl column. This allowed us to extend the molar mass range up to the hundred thousands. As demonstrated in Fig. 1a, PS calibration standards adsorb in 100% DMF on a silica-based octadecyl column. The higher molar mass standards elute later than the lower molar mass standards. The elution volume decreases as the amount of THF in the mobile phase is increased. A point is reached at which all PS standards elute at more or less the same elution volume irrespective of their molar masses (THF–DMF: 18–82). These conditions are called the critical conditions or the CAP of PS for the specific column, temperature and mobile phase conditions used.

Evidence Text: The CAP of PEO was realised on a normal phase Si column in the same mobile phase system (THF–DMF) which is used for the critical conditions of PS. THF is an adsorption promoting solvent for PEOs on a normal phase Si column. The addition of DMF promotes desorption. At a mobile phase composition of 96–4 (THF–DMF, vol.%), all PEO standards elute at more or less the same elution volume and show no molar mass dependence. Further increase in the DMF content in the mobile phase leads to the exclusion of PEOs from pores of the stationary phase resulting in earlier elution of higher molar mass standards than the lower molar mass standards.

Evidence Text: A separation of the non-polar PCL under critical conditions for the polar EO unit can only be possible on reversed phases. On typical RP columns, a CAP for EO exists in 85–95 wt-% methanol–water [60]; in acetone–water two critical points have been observed: at 26–30 wt-% and at 90–93 wt-% (the latter exists only on silica–based stationary phases) [60, 61]. In 90 wt-% methanol–water low molecular PCL samples could be reasonably separated, but higher PCLs are not soluble in such a mobile phase. PCL is also insoluble in 30% acetone, and in 90% acetone the retention of PCL is not sufficient. On RP phases, critical conditions for EO exist also in acetonitrile–water. We have found that PEG and PEG-MME elute close to the CAP in quite a wide range of mobile phase composition: in 69 wt-% acetonitrile–water PEG is sufficiently close to critical conditions (Fig. 1).

Evidence Text: The CAP of PS was realised in a DMF–THF mobile phase on a RP octadecyl column. This allowed us to extend the molar mass range up to the hundred thousands. As demonstrated in Fig. 1a, PS calibration standards adsorb in 100% DMF on a silica-based octadecyl column. The higher molar mass standards elute later than the lower molar mass standards. The elution volume decreases as the amount of THF in the mobile phase is increased. A point is reached at which all PS standards elute at more or less the same elution volume irrespective of their molar masses (THF–DMF: 18–82). These conditions are called the critical conditions or the CAP of PS for the specific column, temperature and mobile phase conditions used.

Corresponding Author: Muhammad Imran Malik; Gareth W. Harding; Monika Elvira Grabowsky; Harald Pasch
Physical Address: Department of Chemistry and Polymer Science, University of Stellenbosch, Stellenbosch, South Africa; H.E.J. Research Institute of Chemistry, International Centre for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS), University of Karachi, Karachi, Pakistan
Evidence Text: The CAP of PEO was realised on a normal phase Si column in the same mobile phase system (THF–DMF) which is used for the critical conditions of PS. THF is an adsorption promoting solvent for PEOs on a normal phase Si column. The addition of DMF promotes desorption. At a mobile phase composition of 96–4 (THF–DMF, vol.%), all PEO standards elute at more or less the same elution volume and show no molar mass dependence. Further increase in the DMF content in the mobile phase leads to the exclusion of PEOs from pores of the stationary phase resulting in earlier elution of higher molar mass standards than the lower molar mass standards.
